The retriever models and the ranker models are often trained independently using logged feedback data, but ranker models may utilize more contextual information for better predictions.
The key difference is that the retriever uses a tree-structured greedy model to retrieve a subset from a large item pool.

The ranker predicts scores for each item within the subset retrieved by the retriever.
The ranker determines the item with the highest score from the subset, which is returned as the prediction result.

In the training of machine learning algorithms, we are constrained to a finite dataset for learning.
The resulting function must generalize effectively beyond the training sample.
Generalization bounds aim to constrain the loss difference between the loss on the training sample and the loss on the test population.
The generalization gap for a set of samples and items S is defined by the difference between the expected loss and the empirical loss.
Minimizing the empirical loss should not impact generalization if the generalization gap can go to 0 with high probability.
An upper bound on the Rademacher complexity can lead to an upper bound on the generalization gap.

Having an upper bound on the Rademacher complexity allows us to have an upper bound on the generalization gap.
